CUBRENT AFFAIRS,

‘Were it not time that Trinity College revived the Spell-
ing Bee? Spelling does not appear to be invariably one
of the strong points of its graduates. His Grace the
Archbishop of Dublin has provided considerable amuse-
ment for the public at the expense of the discredited
school called a university over which Dr. Bigot Traill
presides. Here are a few specimens of words as they ave
spelled by Sweet Boy Graduates of the Garrison Univer-
sity :— Cavaty, deformaties, cists, studed, scrubed,
rubed, accros, wheater, anty-spasmodic, dry-cupped,
sepparating, palpatation, intamately, laiter, presumtive,
throwen.’’ Another list :— Idiopathick, releave, kneal-
ing, femurus (femoris), inflamation, full-seized, thic
(thigh), internall, meat (meet), incission.”  Perhaps tho
state-herring-branders of College Green believe ipﬁho
spell-as-you-pleasa method, and regard these original
efforts as manifestations of individuality.

We trust that “ Antony Trale’’ and “ Proffeccor Tir-
reell ”’ will rush to the rescue of the “repootashun ’’ of
their college, which has suffered so much at the hands
of an “Tdolatrous’’ Archbishop. We have not scen a

-all the spur that Ireland can give it.

'T.hc “ National "’ Bank is evidently no more in love
with tho Irish language than is Brother Goulding’s
railway. Tho Gaelic League of London, which handlez
a good deal of money during its yearly workings, is now
doing business with an English bank. ~ The English bank
negotiates cheques signed in Irish; the * National 7*
Bank, that issues notes with—what we must now .con-
sider a cameo of raimeis as far as the bank is concerned—
Gipe 5o Lpat on them, preferred to lose the account
of the League than consent to do what several Englisk
banks were willing to do.

As carly as April 3rd of last year the manager of the
Charing Cross Branch of the “ National ’’ Bank, in the
course of a letter, wrote:—“If it is proposed to issue
cheques with the payce’s name in Gaelie I shall be glad
if the society will kindly furnish us with an indemnity
against any risk of paying on an incorrect endorsement.
as I regret that we are unable to accept responsibility ix
such cases.”

The Hon. Treasurer of the London Gaelic League.
Dr. J. J. Atteridge, acting on instructions from the
Committee, replied that they were unable to furnish the
suggested indemnity; he further announced that the
League had decided that all its cheques in future would
be signed in Irish. The ““ National >’ Bank declined te
deal with cheques signed in Irish.  Subsequently Dr.
Atteridge called on one of the directors of the bank,
and the latter brought the question before his Board
again. The letter, dated the 1st August, stated the
matter had been reconsidered, but that the bank could
not see its way to alter its previous decision. A month
afterwards the Hon. Secretary of the League wrote to
the “National”” Bank, stating that unless the bank
accepted cheques signed in Irish that the League would
be obliged to transfer its account to another bank. The
“ National,”” however, stood loyal by its Deupts
ultimatum! ““€ipe so Lpag,” An eav.

Some other “Irish’’ banks were then approached,
but without success, and several English banks agreed
to accept cheques signed in Irish! On the 16th Novem-
ber last the League gave the “ National ’’ Bank of the
“€Gine 50 Dpat’ notes yet another chance—but ne
use. So now the Gaelic League of London keeps its
account with an English bank. g

The Dublin County Council declined to pass Mr. °
Field’s resolution that it should refuse to become am
agent for the collection of the Dublin Metropolitan
Police Tax. Without going into the merits of the reso-
lution as it concerns the Dublin County Council, it is
about time that a distinct agitation for the control of
the police force should be started all over Ireland. It
was recently rumoured that the Liberal Government in-
tended to give Ireland control of its police. Whether
there is any foundation for that rumour or not we de
not know, but certainly it is plainly the policy of the
people to agitate for that desirable reform. It is said
that you should not spur a willing horse, but we have
yeb to be convinced that the present Government is a
willing horse; we are decidedly of opinion that it needs
The Government
has done really nothing for Ireland up to this. The
lifting of the Coercion Act, and the stopping of prose-
cutions for the printing of names on carts in Irish, are
vory cheap gifts to the country; like talk, they cost
nothing; unless we get something tangible it might be
as woll that petty persecutions like coercion and fines
and imprisonment for printing one’s name in Irish onm

his caxt were allowed in force. ~We would have a
firmer faith in the Government if they tendered the
country some carnest of their good faith in the shape of
Irish control of the Irish police.

. A Presbyterian of Castleblayney in the exercise of his
rights as a citizen sent his daughter to the local convent
school. That was his business, and he had a legal right
to do so. If he considered tho  obscurant’’ convent
school was a better educational institution than any
competing “saved *’ school, was he not as a Presbyterian
entitled to his private judgment in the matter, even
though his judgment may have trod on the {oes of non-
convent schools? Some “free and independent ' Orange
lambs of Castleblayney got alarmed. The Orangemen
had succeeded in defeating the nefarious plot at Carn-
thall in Tyrone, where an *“ Idolatrous ** sewing instruc-
tress was routed; why should the “lambs’’ of Castle-
blayney lie down without as much as a bleat out of them
whilst & Presbyterian was sending his daughter to a
convent school? An active campaign was started against
the “private judgment '’ of the Presbyterian by some
Protestant defenders of liberty.”” We read in our
contemporary the Dundalk Democrat:—“ One well-

*knewn townsman, who enjoys a large share of Catholic
patronage, dressed himself in his Sunday clothes on a
market day and went out amongst his co-religionists
from the country to tell them of the terrible thing that
had happened in their midst. He, no doubt, felt called
upon by Heaven to champion this type of religious
liberty which refuses to a parent the right to rear his
according to the dictates of his own reason and
conscience.”’ The agitation succeeded and the Presby-
terian gentleman was forced to bow his neck before the
attack of the champions of “liberty.”” The man Coote
of Carnthall should get into communication with these
heroes of this Castleblayney “ glorious victory,”” and the
| result should be a new party which might be called

’} “ The Liberty Boys.””
[ This being Ireland, it is only in the nature of things
d that the idea of an Irish religious celebration in honour

of St. Patrick, after being put in practice in London,

sh 1 hither via—of all places in the world

[ —Belfast ! s what is happening this year. There

i will be Irish sermons in Dublin and other places, but

apparently in Belfast only of Irish towns will there be

a distinctively Irish celebration. It will take place in

St. Patrick’s on March 18th at 7 p.m. The Lord

& Bishop of the diocese will preside, and there will be no

[ word of English from beginning to end of the service.

All that is educationally and militantly Irish in Belfast

will join in the Irish hymns in honour of God and St.

Patrick. Every Gael that is awake in the city has been

practising for weeks past to that end. Various people

are reading various lessons out of the West Belfast vic-

tory in the election. Local Gaelic Leaguers note that

one effect of it has been to rouse the spirit of the Irish.

They seem to be wisely taking advantage of that fact.

~ We hope that St. Patrick’s on Sunday next will be too

small to hold the congregation that will assemble in re-
sponse to their patriotic call.

‘We have received the following official notice from
the badly-needed Catholic Defence Society :—The usual
monthly meeting of the Council of the Catholic Defenco
Society was held on Tuesday, the 6th inst.  Several
gentlemen, being proposed for membership, were ad-
mitted to the society. The treasurer announced a sub-
stantial balance to credit in bank. Several questions
of very practical interest to Catholics were discussed,
and likewise the reports of the ‘committees appointed to
look after the interests of Catholics on the various rail-
ways.

It will be remombered that a correspondent recently
‘quoted in these columns a sentence from a leading article
~ in the “only national ”’ of Cork. “There is a deplor-
able lack of skill and energy,” said our ill-printed con-

porary, “in the marketing of Irish produce of all
inds, and a wholly unaccountablo tendency to obscu-
tism on the part of leading firms in great national

industries.”” Our correspondent then went on to give
some samples of printed ‘‘ words’’ from the very next
column of the “only national,”’ and certainly the list
placed the samples of spelling by some Trinity graduates
completely in tho shade. Our correspondent pointed
out that theso words, or rather alleged words, showed a
deplorable lack of skill in turning out a newspaper, and
ho suggested that as there were so many idle in Cork
that thoe proprictors of the “only national ** should em-
ploy a fow hands to read proofs.

According to a report in onc of last week’s “onl:
national ’’ issues we sce that Father Barrett, of Bally-
noe, wound up a specch as follows: — He did not ima-
gine that any English Government, Tory or Liberal,
would ever make froe grants of lands to the sons of Irish
tenant farmers, but what he would expect any paternal
responsible Government to do was to provide land on
such terms which it would be impossible for such sons
to give under the existing state of competifion.” 5
Now we wonder what was the reverend speaker driving
at? Or perhaps we should keep our wonder solely for
the deplorable lack of skill in turning out a newspaper
displayed by the owner of the “only national.”  We
are mot surprised to learn that Father Barrett sent a
letter of protest to the “only national ” at being made
to appear as one who talked like a fool. In the course
of his letter ho told the “only national ’’ that there
were a number of errors and omissions in his remarks as
reported, “ But the last sentence of my speech, as re-
ported in the Bzaminer, is such an extraordinary medley
of words and such arrant nonsense that I feel bound to
protest against it. I can accept no responsibility for it,
and I would suggest that some enterprising journal wouid
offer a prize to those of its readers who would succeed in
unravelling it.”” Perhaps it is needless to say vhat the
“only national >’ did not insert Father Barretl’s pro-
test.

In ths course of a note on “St. Patrick’s Day in
Savage South Africa,’” a correspondent from Port Eliza-
beth writes us:—“ Port Elizabeth is a long distance
from Ireland. Tt is “a little town round the Cape of
Good Hope,” to which the Leaper referred nearly two
years ago. It cannot be said to be in the neighbourhood
of Ballyvourncy anyway. The Gaels in the town are
fow, and not many things work in their favour. But
despite the great distance frem Ireland, which many
consider amply justifies apathy, despite fewness of num-
bers, and despite unfavourable local circumstances, the
Gaclic Literary Society were instrumental in having the
Rosary in Irish on last St. Patrick’s Day. This year it
will not be allowed to drop. They are again organising
it, aided of course by the renewed kindness of the Right
Rev. Bishop MacSherry, and the hearty co-operation of
our Irish priests. The Rosary in Irish, with the usual
prayers and the Litany of St. Patrick, followed by
Benediction, will be recited by Father MacAlinney in
St. Augustine’s Church on the afternoon of Sunday,
March 18th.  The Gaels of this town may therefore
fairly lay claim to bo marching with the times, though
in a rear battalion perhaps.  All they regret is that their
celebration is on such modest, lines. They ave glad to
show, however, that many of Eire’s scattered sons ave
not unmindful of her past.”

The drama of “Red Hugh; or, Life and Death of
THugh Roe O'Donnell,”” by T, O. Russell, was acted
with great success by the Irish Catholic students in
Pavis Tast month. Tt will be acted by the students of
8t. Patrick’s Collego, Carlow, on St. Patrick’s Day.
The Cork National Theatre Socioty that acted “The
Last Trish King”’ in Cork last year hope to act ¢ Red
Hugh " next autumn, or carly “in the winter.

We suspect there are still many of the Dark Brother-
hood' who are yet to come into tho light of day. It
was only a week or two ago that we were reminded of
the fact that there was an Irish firm manufacturing
combs. In one of the early numbers of the LeapEr we
believe we published such information, but the matter
had passed from our recollection when a representative
of the firm recently called on us and reforred to it.
However, Messrs. A. Mitchell do actually manu-
facture combs in Dublin, and having overcome the
shyness that made them avert their gaze from the public
for so mauny years, we have no doubt they will not re-
gret this introduction to the Irish Ireland portion of it
which we are glad to give them.

The Language Procession last Sunday was a fine turn
out, and that despite the wretched weather. The
weather was wretched, and we have no doubt that many
who took part in the great demonstration are now laid
up with colds. The worst of it is that the weather
difficulty will always be a danger and obstacle in the
month of March: even if the rain keeps away it is
almost sure to be bitterly cold, and many tableaux and
things of that kind are only possible subject to many
difticulties. Certainly fhe shifting of the event from
the Sunday before St. Patrick’s Day to the Sunday be-
fore the Oireachtas week is matter for serious discussion.
Even as a collecting machine the Procession in March
could not, we should say, come near a Procession in
August; and there is no reason why, if the date were
shifted, that a collection should not remain a feature
of the Procession.

An important point, of cowrse, is—would the absence
of the Frocession in Dublin on the Sunday before St.
Patrick’s Day militate against the collection throughout
the country during Language week? If it would of
course that would be a strong reason against changing
it, for, after all, the collection is the main concern of
Language Week. We don’t see any reason to think that
it would. Indeed we would suggest that if March is to
be retained as the month of the procession the date
ought to be changed from the Sunday before to the Sun-
day after St. Patrick’s Day. A sort of reaction inevit-
ably sets in after the occurrence of any great event to
which people have been looking forward. We suggest
that the Language collection should commence the Sun-
day before St. Patrick’s Day and that Language Week
should wind up with the great event in the streets of
Dublin on the following Sunday—that is if August is
ruled out. While people have a crowning event to look
forward to their interest can easily be kept in a state of
stimulation for a week, and it is whilst their interest is
in that state that the money boxes should be rattled
before their faces, and that the house to house collection
should ba made. When the week commences with what
for the ordinary Dublin public is the crowning event,
well then there is, we fear, a temptation to lose intercst
in the matter as a thing past and gone.

The Procession was a great success, and the Industrial
section was again one of its chief features. But after
all the Gaelic League does not exist to make a cheap
holiday for Dublin sightseers. ~The Procession is over,
but, the main business of the work of Language Week,
which is the collection of funds for carrying on the
Organisation, is by no means over. And we trust our
readers, who are nearly all either Gaelic Leaguers or
sympathisers with the general Irish Ircland idea, will,
during the week, subscribe liberally to the funds.

Tho seventh annual meeting of the Cork Branch of
tho so-called “National’”” Union of Shop Assistants,
ete., was held one day last week. The thing, of course,
is about as national as a Grafton Street shop window.
The chairman rogretted that a Mr. Fludson, M.P., an
English Parliamentarian, was not present. But the
British M.P. wrote a letter. The chairman at this
meeting in €ork, where William comes from, was glad
to say that the Union had now for the first time a direct:
representabive in Parliament in the person of Mr. J. A.

Seddon, Newtown Division of Lancs.! What are “Mr.
Augustino Roche and mysclf * suppuzed to bo doing on
the floor, we wonder! Mr, Egan, Fresident of the Cork
Trades, is not satisfied with the present incernational
limitations of this union. According to the “only na-
tional ” of Cork, this Cork internationalist said p=
“One thing he regretted was that the amalgamation
was not more general, and he hoped some day to sce
Burope, Asia, America and Australia amalgamated on
the question of labour.”” He referred to the distinctly
Trish body, the Irish Drapers’ Assistants’ Union, and he
hold—-they always do—that any man of the Interna-
tional organisation present was as good an lrishman as
any member of the Irish Drapers’ Assistants’ Union!
Mr. Stewart, who, we believe, opposed fhe closing of
publichouses in Dublin on St. Patrick’s Day, delivered
an address on “Shop Life in the Great Cities: Its De-’
velopment and Conditions.””  We don’t read that he
said anything about the shop life of Bung’s Bond Slaves
on St. Patrick’s Day. Towards the couclusion of the
meeting Mr. Egan, who hoped to see Europe, Asia, Ame-
rica and Australia amalgamated on the question of
labour, mentioned the fact that the National monument
would be unveiled on St. Patrick’s Day !

We have received the first number of “ Irish Garden-
ing,”’” a new monthly jowrnal. As its name implies, it is
devoted to the interests of Trish horticulture. The first
number contains an interesting article on potatoes, a
comparison of English and Trish types. — Mr. Moore,
Director of the Botanic Gardens, Glasnevin, d in an
article with the present position of horticulture in Ire-
land, and Sir Horace Plunkett gives the venture a send
oft in the shape of a letter of approval, enclosing sub-
seription.  The first number also contains various other
practical contributions,

If the Industrial Revival on the manufacturers’ side
depended on such firms as the Killaloe Slate Quarries
the industrial outlook would be none too hopeful. This
industry got a great fillip from the LEADER propaganda,
and its earnings went up. In 1902 they had made so
well out of the Industrial Revival that a bonus of £60
was voted to the directors; though commenting on the
fact at the time we suggested that the LeapEr had more
call on that bonus than the directors. In an article by
“ A Disgusted Sharcholder,” which appeared in our
issue of March 29th, 1902, our contributor said, “ Could
not some enterprising meu form a company who would
buy out or fire out the present directors.””

On last year’s working the Killaloe Dark Brothers
made a net profit of £5 8s. 3d. We do not read that
anyone proposed that the £5 should be given by way of
bonus to the dircctors, and that the odd shillings and
pence should be divided amongst the sharcholders. In-
deed some of the sharcholders had the unmitigated im-
pudence to snarl at the heaven-sent directors of Kil-
lalos Dark Brotherhood, and turn up their noses at the
year’s profit of £5 8. 3d.! A nasty “committes of
sharcholders ’’ has been appointed—such a committee as
was unsuccessfully agitated for in connection with that
other depreciated property over which the  gentleman’’
presides.  Unanimously, the following proposal was
adopted : — That this meeting stand adjourned until a
full inquiry is made into the working of the quarries by
a committes of sharcholders, the committee to inquire
into the trading of the company generally, and to report
to the extraordinary meeting to be held on tha 4th April
noxt.”’

At the meeting Mr, R. P. Gill, C.E., who lives near
the quarries and knows them intimately, dealt vigorously
with the plight of the company. “ THas there been any
effort made to sell their stock on hands, either by adver-
tising or by any employee of the company trying to in-
duce 1m=embers of the public to purchase it?’ he asked.
That was cartainly a case of bearding the Dark Brothers
in their den! Tt is a wonder that the divectors did not
fling a f b Mr. Gill when he uttered th> dread-
ful werd A :

Eike the Great Sick and Sourface Railway, the Killa-

- B2 Park Brothers imported their manager. Mr. Gill
said :—“ There are men engaged in the place for very
mazny years, some of them nearly all their lives there,
and they have developed an instinct which enables
ghem to know where to trace those rocks, but these
men have been put aside to a certain extent, and this
manm, with nothing like that experience, has been
placed over them at a salary of—I want to know what,
as it is not disclosed in the report.”” Mr. Gill also
said:—“Tt would be hopeless to expect the directors
2a do anything, because they know absolutely nothing
about it. They are men who never knew a blessed
thing about quarries or their working. They always
‘Bad a good supply of means and moncy, and never
#axed their minds trying to understand matters of that
mature. They are the worst type of men to control a
woncern of this kind. They would be excellent men,
-m0 doubt, to be at the head of a benevolent institu-
#on, but to be over a solid business undertaking they
ava absolutely useless.’”

Now one of the directors is a Mr. Tuthill, and he, no
doubt, felt that he was called upon to make some de-
flemca to the onslaught which Mr. Gill made on the
garticular kind of slates that roofed the brains of the
directors. Mr. Tuthill’s retort was a gem of uncon-
mious humour. He said to Mr. Gill:—“ You must re-
mmember that although I am a country gentleman I have
Been a gold medalist in college, and you can hardly
say I am perfectly incompetent to deal with figures.”
Did ever anyone hear such a remark from a director
of an industrial company? We wonder was it at
“Trinity *’ that he got the gold medal? Mr. Gill
aptly retorted :—“ But I am referring to the working
2% the quarry. Winning a gold medal in college does
=0t make a man qualified for managing a business con-
zern.”” We look forward with interest to the report
#hat will be submitted to the meeting on April 4th
;ext.

The three Bills now being promoted by the Great Nor-
thern Bigot Railway Co. have been blocked by YMr.
MacVeagh, Mr. P. O'Brien, and Mr. Swift MacNeill,
It will be remembered that at the last half-year_lv
meeting the chairman of this notorious company said
that the candidates for clerkships were examined by
persons in the employment of the company, who knew
what was required. Well, the three Bills of the com-
pany are now being blocked by persons who know what
is required. When the motion of Mr. Dolan, of Ardee,
was put at the general meeting last month the chairman
said the “ Noes’’ had it, though it appeared to many
that the “ Ayes’’ had it, and the chairman refusedl a
poll though a poll was demanded. Now the three Bills
being promoted by this narrow and intolerant Board of
Directors are being blocked. When Brother Goulding’s
Company is promoting a Bill in Parliament he, though
“ Grand Steward ’’ of the Free and Accepted Masons of
Treland, may find similar impediments in his path.

“FASHION” AND THE INDUSTRIAL REYIYAL.

Yet another annual meeting of the “society ’’ play-
thing by the name of the “ Royal Irish Industries As-
sociation ** was held recemtly at Lincoln Place. These
sort of “society '’ movements—if they may be called
movements—make as much noise as an empty barrel,
and get a great deal more notice in the Press than their
meagre merits deserve. Just as Mr. Bung may like te
put a little ointment on his troubled conscience by sub-
scribing a sovereign to a charity, a great many of the
landlord class in this country—those people who have
been such a curse to it, and such a burden on it—like
to toy with a “ Royal’’ Irish Industry Society, and en-
deavour to persuade themselves that they are doing
something by way of justifying their—as far as Ireland
is concerned—worse than useless existence. And, of
course, where a countess is there shall mesdames on the
climb be gathered together. Lady Aberdeen takesa great
interest in this industrial plaything. We freely give her
the credit of meaning well in the matter; but if she
really would do work for Irish industry, she will have te
do something else than be a figure-head for the ladies
of varying degree who flit round her at 21 Lincoln
Place. :

Lady Aberdeen could do a real stroke of work
for Irish industry if she wished to do so. Many branches
of industry are kept alive by that very potent force
which we call “ fashion.”” Fashion has various sources,
but in the main it is set, if not always created, by those
who are “up’’ in the social scale. As currents run at
present, and probably will continue to run as long as
the world is a world, you might as well attempt to alter
the law of gravitation as change that fact. Those om
top have the power to set a fashion ; those on the ground
floor can only attack fashion by agitation. ~ “ Upper
circles’’ of some kind or other, like the poor, we will
have always with us, and they should be used for ah
they are worth. The people have been agitating, and
agitating to some purpose, on behalf of Irish manufac-
ture. - It would be no harm if those “ mighty ones™
whose privilege it is not to be “ the people,”” used their
powers in the way of setting the fashion Irishwards. To
como to a concrete suggestion. Wiy should not Lady
Aberdeen, ab the next open-air function that she gives—
garden party or whatever it may be—announce that she
will be dressed in a costume made in Ireland of Irish
material, boots made in Ireland, an Irish straw hat,
trimmed in artificial flowers, made in Ireland, that she
shall carry a sun-shade or an umbrella, as the weather
may require, made in Ircland, and that her gloves will
bo Irish; and further, that she wishes, desires, com-
mands, or whatever the proper word may be for a Vice-
vogal hint, that all the ladies attending the function
chould bo attired in a manner as Irish as herself?

‘Such a move on the part of Lady Aberdeen would
create a greater offect in a week than the “ Royal Irish
Tndustries”’ toy of Lincoln Place would do in a life-

¥R. DAVITT’'S EDUCATION IDOLS—PHILOSOPHY
IN SWADDLING CLOTHES.—VI.

I showed last week, not on my aubthority, nor yet on
that of Mr. Daviit, what is thought by non-Catholics in
America of the public school system there, and on the
authority of non-Catholic Frenchmen whatis the result of
the State school system in France. Mr. Davitt assures us
that the secular school system is exactly the right thing,
that it works splendidly wherever it has been tried. In
the days of raimels we would open our mouths and swal-
low his words as a matter of course. We would not
think of asking ourselves—I wouder is he quite correct?
What special information has he on this subject? That
was twenty years ago. But times have changed since
then, and we have changed with them. Within the past
few years, if a man like Mr. Davitt, in a speech, or
through a few acres of a newspaper letter, pours out
“ democracy *’ in mouthfuls, like a lava flow from Vesu-
vius, we examine whether it be real lava, or only smoke
and ashes; whether it be genuine democracy, or only
autocracy under the other name; whether it be only
raimets that palavers or reason that persuades. Raimews
is easily seen through once it is tested, because, like all
froth, a breath can blow it away. To call in question
the words of a popular politician on any subject, from
religion to reading and writing, is of course very provok-
ing; it is so very new. But that is how we do to-day;
and even Mr. Davitt will have to bend to the galling
yoke like the rest of us common units of democracy.
Mr. Davitt is not accustomed to have his words ques-
tioned, and hence he has got very vexed; but he will get
used to it in time. Ile seems to be still living in the
Ireland of twenty years ago.

In that it hava I enquired of non-Catholic autho-

rities rica and France, if what Mr. Davitt tells
us of the secular system is correct ; and my readers will
remember that their evidence flatly contradicts what he
tells us. They talk facts, Mr. Davitt talks poetry. The
difference between Mr. Davitt and them is—They speak
from personal trial, he speaks from hearsay ; they are on
the spot and speak what they know, he speaks what he
plainly does not know. I am sure he feels quite satisfied
that he knows—but he doesn’t. Looking at the matter
as plain people, we cannot take his words at his own
value in face of what, those men tell us. Let Mr. Davitt,
then, still keep thinking that he is right—granted, that’s
his share in the democracy; but the public will go on
thinking otherwise—quite right too; that’s their de-
mocracy. Mr. Davitt secems to think that in what he
calls “ the rising democracy,’” his think should be taken
as the democratic think. Oh, no, Mr. Davitt—The de-
mocracy is rising fast, and I for one heartily hail its
coming; but in it, the democratic think shall be the
people’s think, in which I, of course, include all, rich
and poor, priest and politician. Mr. Davitt’s idea of
democracy is, according to what he said to the Orange-
men of Belfast the other day, that the priest shall have
no voice in the public affairs of his country in a self-
governing Ireland. Indeed! And yet shall he have to
pay rates and taxes, and hold his tongue? That is M.
Davitt’s democracy. If your think were to bo taken for
their think, it would not be democracy but autocracy ;
and if you want the people to accept your think, without
doing any thinking for themselves, you are not a demo-
crat at all, but a demagogue wearing the wrong label on
your hat,

I have made those few remarks on democracy and in-
dependent thinking ; they hold a useful light to us whilst
we check Mr. Davitt’s hearsay assurances by the testi-

- whom T have brought on the witness table to contradict
him. Let him cross-examine them, if he dare.

As to the Board School system in England, T repeat—
Before 1870, the only provision made in England for the
oducation of the poor was in Voluntary Denominational
Schools, the Catholics maintaining their own schools
entirely at their own expense, and out of their poverty.
At that time, did the Secularists found and support
voluntary secular schools at their expense for the educa-
tion of the poor? Not a bit of it; nor for the edudation
of anyone else. Did the Nonconformists do so? Not
thoy, bless you! The Secularists and the Nonconform-
ists cadged their education at the voluntary schools built
and maintained out of the pockets of others. They have
carned a similar name for themselves in Australia. It
rominds one of the professional diner-out who appears,
in evening dress of course, as an habitual guest at the
tablo of others, takes his victuals and wash-down with
the airs of a < gentleman,”” and talks superior to the
long-suffering host at whoso expense and table he feeds.
In those days over 1,000,000 children of the poor were
running wild over England without a school. The Ca-
tholics had nobly provided schools for their poor out of
their poverty. The Anglicans had their own voluntary
schools, although I must say that out of their wealth
they might have done much more; they have shown
little sacrifice and little principle in this matter. But
did the Secularists support any voluntary secular school
to help in civilising those 1,000,000 poor children who
were running wild? Not one. Thus, when interest in
the education of the poor had to be proved by the pocket,
and cost cash down, neither the voice of the Secularist
nor the jingle of his guinea was heard ; now when that
interest costs little more than talk, when Secularism,
with the Nonconformist religion sneaked in, has been
intruded into the schools, and Catholics made to contri-
bute to both, their voice in the educational interest of
the poor is the loudest in the land. But every hollow
sound, however loud, soon passes; but the voice of com-
mon sense keeps telling us that after all “the proof of
the pudding is in the eating of it.”” That is how the
Sccularists proved their pudding.

Mr. Davitt tells us that the State has the first right
in the education of the child; and according to him,
even the parent of the child must give place, and play
second fiddle. He will hear more on that point later on.
But just here I want to say that if it be the first right of
the State, it was also its first duty to provide for the edu-
cation of the children. How was this duty done before
1870, when the State contributed only a few miserable
thousands a year towards that purpose? And how was
it dono before 1838, when it gave not a copper at all?
How was that duty done in America up to fifty years
ago, when the State in democratic America did as little
for the education of the child, as it did in monarchical
England? What about the State’s first right to educate
the child—where was it then, Mr. Davitt? Every right
is founded on a duty ; and if the State had the first right,
it had the first duty also, but who did the duty then, Mr.
Davitt? Oh! no first rights of the State were clamoured
for in those days by philosophers in swaddling clothes.
All that timo the Seculavists looked on idly whilst
others laboured. But, in Amevica fifty years ago, in
England 30 years ago, those who had done nothing and
paid nothing to create or maintain voluntary seculan
schools of their own in those countries, came forth, with
the proverbial impudence of those who neither do nox
pay, rending the air with their cry of sympathy for the
school-less poor, decrying the education provided by
those who had done everything and paid everything, and
took oub a new podagogical patent for producing a new
generation of citizens with more strenuous civic virtues
and higher moral lives. And what sort of article has
this patent school factory supplied to the State? We
havo already heard the terrible tale from compotent wit-
nesses in Australia, America and France. And how has
it beon in England? I know from personal knowledge
that the eccontricities of the London School Board are
the laughing-stock of educationists. The school rates
raised and the money spont are enormous, and are rising
yearly—squandered ‘on oducational hobbies, ridden out
of breath for the season, and then cast aside for equally
oxtravagant ones next yoar.  If any persons should
think I go boyond bounds, let them get the iSchool-

of a photograph. But, adopting his topsy-turvy view
of the matter, he makes a great mistake in thinking
that teachers have an impartial field of work in a system
of education under public control. I know that Catho-
lic teachers find their religion a hindrance to them, both
as to appointment and as to promotion, in most English
Board Schools. I know that nominally those posi-
tions and promotions are open to all; but so are posi-

yebt somehow Catholics miss getting tho job. I know
from Catholic teachers that they suffer the same disabi-

The Irish World, the New York Freeman’s Journal, and
other American papers, that Catholic teachers suffer
equally in the States. I showed from M. Goyan’s article

that a Catholic teacher in the French State schools is
spied upon, and if found to be a practical Catholic is
doomed to an inferior position or to dismissal, in spite

It may be all correct in theory, but that is the thing in
practice—in democratic France and America, as well as
in monarchical Saxonland. I am inclined to think that
Mxr. Davitt, idealising away in his study about men and
things, is much too innocent for this sinful world in
which we live.

‘Thus, the State which, according to Mr, Davitt, has
tthe first right in the education of the child even to the
sxclusion of the parents’ right, relinquished its right
.and grossly neglected its duty for a very long time—in
fact, until recent years. If the priest and the parent

dered on educational experiments, and in turning the

There are at this day about 70,000 children running
about the streets of New York without a school to go t0;
thero is no school accommodation for them. Why does
the State neglect its duty to those waifs and strays? If
their education is its first right, it must be its first duty

in mind that the Catholics of New York City, after help-
ing the Stato to support the State schools, provide school

right of the State in educating the child is paramount
to that of the parent, as Mr. Davitt tells us—which of
course is a monstrous outrage in parental rights—the
State has been grossly neglecting its duty.

This right and duty of education sits very lightly on
the conscience of secularists, so long as only education is
concerned ; but when the opportunity comes to divorce
religion from the schools they shame parents in their
interest for the parent’s child, and turn the educational

ligion is divorced is applicable only to the poor. The

of the poor schools, maintained by public money, must
have no religion taught them in school. If their parents
want it for them, let them teach them their catechism
at six in tho morning before they go to work, or at night
when they come home after the labours of the day, or
let the children start for school an hour or two before

their poverty. That is roughly Mr. Davitt’s system ;

then Mr. Forde has to pay for it; it costs Mr. Davitt

our patriotic support. - Anyhow, our Temperance re-
| formers might well make use of the evidence adduced
at said trial to impress on people the folly of spending
money on whiskey at all. The case having been so re-
cently heard the points are fresh in the public mind.
For the benefit of the “man in the street,”” however,
the setting down of a few elementary facts about the
drink traffic may serve a useful purpose just now.
First and foremost, up to a few years ago, whiskey
was made in the old-fashioned way by what is known
as the “pot still.”” The process was natural, and was
slow, just as the natural process of getting the cream
off milk was slow. The inventor, however, was about,
and, in due course, what is known as the “ patent still ’
was invented, and came into vogue. This instrument
literally makes whiskey “ while you wait,”’ just as the
separator skims milk while you wait, and in addition
makes it out of almost any sort of ingredient such as
maize, rye, potatoes, etc. In a way, this “ patent still”’
whiskey is to pure whiskey what margarine is to butter,
but with this difference that margarine can be proved
not to be butter, but “ patent’’ spirit cannot, so far,
be satisfactorily distinguished chemically from pure or
pot-still whiskey. Thus, the result of the introduction
of the patent still was that this inferior stuff was put
into competition with the honest whiskey without it
being in anybody’s power to prove chemically that it
was not honest whiskey. What an opportunity such a
state of affairs afforded the spirit trader to palm off the
bad for the good is obvious. Here is how it was done—
done scientifically and legally : —
. Belfast is the moral capital of Ireland, we all know
that. Well, the whiskey-blenders there early saw the
possibilities of the patent still. By law every cask of
i1 whiskey distilled has to be marked with a progressive
it - number, and also with the year of distillation.  The
- Excise authorities see to that, so that if the whiskey
were allowed to remain in the original casks, everybody
could see for himself what age the stuff was. When
these original casks are blended, however, they are
newly marked with the year of blending, and the mer-
chant may then mark them in addition any way he
likes—* Very old,” “TFinest old Irish,” “Special old
.Scotch,” or such like, and the purchaser, seeing the
cask marked hlended, thinks that the whiskey is roally
a3 old as it is described. Thus, with the patent still
to give him the very cheapest of whiskey to blend, and
with the law, as it stands, to enable him to disguise the
“age, and to describe as he likes the quality of tho stuff
“he has for sale, the blender can simply cheat the public
as‘much as he likes. As was proved at the trial the
common practice is to blend this patent spirit with 10
per. cent, or so of pure pot-still whiskey, and to describo
and sell the blend as Irish or Scotch, as “ Finest old
Irish,”” or as “ Very Finest old Scotch,” or whatever
~ else you like. I may say here that this practico it is
that has ruined the good name Irish whiskey once had.
‘People over the wator, when continually sold this red-
ot patent spirit for “ special Irish,”’
the special Irish was deteriorating.  The decision the
_ other day has shown what was the matter. Belfast
‘had been ‘oo virbuous civically. It is now recognising
that it killed the goose that was laying the golden oggs,

That is a general statement of the case as to the
whiskey traffic; it may interest those who are not Antis
Troators if I follow a particular cask of whiskey into the
bar-parlour of a public-house. The whiskey is. magle,
as T have said, to-day by tho patent process, of Russian
rye suppose, or bad potatoes. It comes off red-hot, and
is so comparatively worthless—for it is on the yeast that O
is got during the distillation that most of the distiller’s
pr:ﬁL is made—that it is sold and delivered carriage
paid at as low as 10d. (ten pence) per gallon at 25 pex
cont. over proof. This means Lh.z'Lt out of every gallon
of whiskey at this strength, 13 gallons of ordinar
public-house whiskey may be obtained by the addition
of water. That is to say, the trader pays 10d. for a
oallon of this patent whiskey, he pays 13s. 9d. duty,
i'oml 14s. 7d., and he sells 17 gallons over the counter
at 4d. per glass; that is, he gets about 355._ 'for what ho
paid 13s. 9d. in duty, and 10d. for the stuff itself! Nor
is this all. The whiskey, as I have said, is generally
blended, and marked with any description the trader
likes. When so marked a puncheon of it is taken to the
public-house, and hoisted upstairs. Below in the bar
you see the casks with the shining hoops and cocks. One Y
of them is marked “10 years old—5d. per glass,” the
next “7 year old—4}d. per glass,” and so on down to
the cheapest, which 1s rarely less than “3 years old.””
The first cask—stock casks they are called—is filled out
of our friend, the puncheon, upstairs; the second is
filled out of same puncheon; the third ditto, and so on,
and then said puncheon is sent back for another con-
signment of “Finest old Cream Irish Whiskey,”” or
Scotch, as the case may be.

It will be asked, if this be the case why hasn’t it been
found out before now? That’s just what those in the
know are wondering at. It will also be asked is there
no pure whiskey being made or sold in public-houses?
There is. There are distillers who make honest whiskey,
and who jealously guard their property and the public,
but in general the blenders “run’’ the business, or
rather have been running and ruining it. There will
probably be legislation immediately dealing with the
whole question, but it is not in human nature, Britisk 2
human nature, to expect that a Government that gets
13s. 9d. duty on 10d. worth of stuff will be very cruel
1:0 the trade that pays so well for permission to be let
1ve.

In conclusion, is there any good in hoping that our
immortal people will, even now, give up the habit of
treating each other with stuff the value of which, in pro-
portion to the duty, is about 1 to 15. Talk about the 3
Tithes!  Or is there any good in putting it to those
flaiteamail people who pay 10s. a gallon for fire-water -
for wakes, that it is not decency to pour such stuff down
poor people’s throats, not patriotism to pay 8s. 3d. per
gallon to the nefarious British Treasury for the privi-
lege of being allowed to do so.

3 TRALEE.
HE capital of the County Kerry can compare
favourably, for its size, with any town in Ire-
land for the industry and business capacity of its inhabi-
tants. It has maintained its population for the past few
decades wh}le that of most other towns in Treland was
on the decline; this fact considering the turmoil caused
by the Land League struggle, and the agitation and
restlessness which characterised the County Kerry during
the years of its progress, speak strongly for the OIICl'n;
(_)f the Tralee people in maintaining their ground z\n:l
in fact, extending and improving in cvcr; (lircciiou——’
for Tralee has been much improved in appearance during
the past twenty years. The credit for the business en.
terprise which has kept the population together must
be given to the leading merchants of the Lm\?n, of whose
capacity one cannot speak too highly—the McCowens
Donova}]s, Latchfords, Slatterys, Kellihers, and Ruvin":
tons, give considerable employment, and ave just tho
sort of men that would make any town go ahead. It is
not, however, to show what a prosperous town Tralee is
that I am inditing this article, buf to see if any remarks
of mine would have any influence in awakening its inha-
bitants to a sense of what is due from them to the Irish
Lauguage Movement, which has taken such strong hold
in many parts of Ireland, especially the city of Dublin,
but is, T regret to say, considered of little or no conse-
quence in the enterprising town of Tralee—the capital
of tha Irish-speaking County of Kerry.

If I were asked what is doing in Tralee for the ad-
vancement of the language T would answer—Very little.
True there is a branch of the Gaelic League there, but
their efforts will never come to anything until the apathy
which prevails outside is removed, and a hearty support
given by those from whom it should be expected, namely,
all who wish to be known as Irish and not West Britons.
The present spirit of indirference is disheartening, and
there may be some excuse for it if the town was without,
a good Irish teacher; but the Gaelic League has for a

THE LEADER. 59

good while past secured the services of Mr. James O’Sul-
livan—a very competent man and a good native speaker,
so there is no excuse on that score. The truth is that
there is no interest in the language taken in the town.
You have, as in many towns, a fow earnest men who
would like to seo things go the right way, but what can
bo dono .against a mountain of indifference. I admit
we have had a Feis in the town for the past few years,
and that subseriptions were forthcoming for the event,
bub that does not necessarily mean that all those sub-
scribing were interested, for, I am afraid in nfany cases,
he subscriptions were given more to please the person
collecting than from any desire to benefit the object.
The credit for keeping the Feis up must be given to the
few carnest men abovc referred to, for it is no easy mat-
ter under tho circumstances. 2

There are some very good societies in the town where
young men meet. The Tralce Catholic Literary Society,
the Foresters, Young Ireland, and Temperance Socie-
ties, where they could form classes of their own if they
did not care to join the Gaelic League Branch. A
strong clacs for the young ladies of the town could also
be had if the right national spirit prevailed—the old
saying when there’s a will there’s a way is strongly ap-
plicable here.

Making a comparison of the work that is doing in a

I HAVE been listening to people discussing the article

entitled “ Art-Tasters.”” They werce pleased for
the most part; they felt that some people “ whom they
never read’’ were getting it “hot.”” Perhaps they
felt that “art’’ was at last coming down to their own
level. They did not discuss as to whether the article
was not built upon a misconception ; yet such seems to
me to be the thing worth discussing; and about this
misconception it may do some little good to say some
simple words.

The burden of the article is this:—Wo are allowed
to discuss literature, even dramatic literature, why not
pictures? This is the theme. Hero is a sentence in
which it is crystalized. “ However, they (the critics)
invariably sink their differences, and join in a unani-
mous howl against any rash outsider—who can talk
freely and intelligently of Dante, Shakespeare, Cer-
vantes, Thackeray, and other literary masters—if he
should say anything about a painted picture. The art-
tasters form a gang or combine and say, ‘ Out, Philis-
tine, out.”’”” Now I will have served my immediate
purpose if I show that the power of talking intelligently
about Dante’s ““ Hell ’ does not connote a similar power
with regard to Angelo’s “ Judgment.”’

The man who speaks intelligently about Dante—who
is he? How has he got to that stage? He began far
back in his youth, perhaps with “ Pete the Pirate.”” At
that time Hamlet, if the youth ever happened to make
his acquaintance, was but a ghost or an adumbration
compared with the aforesaid Pete. From the pirate
stage he crept up gradually, battening on what now, in
his Shakespeare stage he regards as the veriest trash,
climbing in his progress over such things as “ Cork

Weekly Examiners,”’ Shamrocks,”” ¢ Strand Magazincs,””
“ Claude Duvals.”” It is such hard journeying that
but few, very few, ever get to the Delectable Mountains,
Think of the thousands who read 7'it-Bits even in man-
hood! Nor do those few get to the top without help.
There is criticism all round, there are art-tasters all
round, ever reminding us of the uplands, and we in our
petulance did, I suppose, in our own time, often
turn on such as we met, snarling: Let me
alone, let me read what I like! There was tra-
dition occasionally helping us—the most powerful aid
of all, the thing that made it easy for the illiterate
of the old Medizmval cities to understand what was beau-
tiful, what was art; the thing that almost made it
impossible for them to create an un-beautiful thing when
they became craftsmen. All these things were helping
us upwards to the Shakespeare stage; more than all
these, we had the means of climbing. Shakespeare can
be had for a shilling and the Dante I first read was
purchased complete for sixpence.

These were the external -aids, now for the internal
aid, that is, the use of a material by the poet with which
we are familiar. The man who speaks the meanest
thought is a fellow-worker with Shakespeare. It is in
degree that their work differs. Shakespeare spoke of
life in words; and every day we gossip of the things
happening round us.

Here then we have the aids by means of which one
comes to be able to speak intelligently of Shakespeare’s
work. Thero is the gradual education in literature,
there is criticism, there is the fact that we are familiar
with the material in which Shakespeare worked. Now,
contrast all this with the pains which the average man
takes to understand pictures. Take it that he
lives in Dublin. He is taught little or no drawing in
school (I know all about teaching drawing in schools) ;
he absolutely learns no more about colour than a man
who never heeds literature learns about words. He does
not bother going to an art-school; he has to do short-
hand (he is cultured in a manner natural to his sphere
of usefulness). Yet colour and form, of which he
knows next to nothing, are the big things in pictures!
He would, of course, assimilate a certain amount of
what, for convenience, I may call art-culture (though I
cannot think of culture existing apart from an intimate
knowledge of art) if he were surrounded by beautiful
things, for it was thus that the old craftsman became
cultured, but it is the condition of his time and life
not to be so surrounded, instead he is surrounded by
things the very reverse of beautiful. How often
does he visit the National Gallery? George B. Shaw
has recorded that it is his idea that he himself is the
only Irishman who has ever gone through it! I know
myself that it is almost always empty. And when he
docs visib the Gallery, does he carry it about in his
head afterwards, does he carry with him from it even
4 memory of a single harmony of colour, a memory of
any combination of curves, a memory of any subtle face
expression,  He is bothered instead of rejoiced.
To put it blank, is he not like the boy
dropping his “ Pete the Pirate’” to dip for the nonce
into “The Winter’s Tale?”” Does the dip do him any
good? Well, if he dip often enough, he may begin
to have qualms of conscience about the grandeur of
Pete, but if he dip but once in a way there is no fear
that Peto’s ship shall sink through scuttling. No, as
a rule the odd visit to a gallery of pictures does the
man no good; ho reads a poem and he %as the poem;
ho sees the pictures, but he has not the picture (as &
matter of fact he does not see the picture).

This latter point may not be believed, but it is none

raphrase it variously, in some instances just merely
serving the likeness and interpolating thoughts of
my own, and if I, putting these various versions of the
passage before a man, find him choosing the one least

of trees, make, by mixing one colour with another,
various tints more or less like the colour of the clump,
I know from practical experience that the average '’
man will choose the one most wrong. It is so \aviLh
every colour in the landscape. How can such a man
see & landscape picture?  Perhaps it would be nearer
to the paraphrase example if I were to make various
drawings of a face in a picture; here also I am just as
sure that the worst copy would be accepted; for that
copy which would exaggerate the most would be the
accepted one.

Returning; he who speaks intelligently about Shakes-
peare has assimilated Shakespeare ‘and literature
generally, he must also have the power of understanding
life; and just as surely he who would appreciate Tur-
ner sufficiently to be able to talk intelligently about his
works, must have assimilated Turner and painting
generally, and must understand the life of the land-
scape, as well, its ways of expressing its emotions, how
it looks when it is frost-bound, how when the birds
begin to sing in spring-time; how it looks when calm-
ness is on its features, and how when anger. Where is
your average man? What are clouds to your ordinary
man ?

Now, I brought in how Shakespeare used words as
his material—a material with which we are all familiar;
why did I bring this fact in? To show how naturally
man develops towards understanding Shakespzare rather
than Turner. Man has various ways of expressing his
thoughts; the general way is by means of words. But
he has other ways; and in these other ways he can
think. He can think in colour, in form, in sounds.

Turner thought in colour—uhis was the material with
which he created (this word will serve very well). I do
not believe that all painters think in colour alone when
creating pictures; subject-painters (a legitimate class,
I hold) use both colour and words, the latter mostly as
a stimulating power. We all have these various powers
of thinking, but we allow them to lie dormant. It is the
fault of our education—a fault seldom dwelt upon.

To sum up: the man knows little or nothing about
form and colour ; he never makes “ painting "’—its laws,
its limitations, the clemental power of colour to express
emotion, etc., a subject of thought, that is a part: of his
being; he cannot even think as the artist thinks; he
cannot even sece the picture—no wonder that he often-
times prefers the bizarre and the worthless to the beau-
tiful and good.

To finish, I hope I have shown that the “average
man cultured according to his sphere of usefulness, can-
not speak as intelligently about Turner as Dante, unless
he take the same pains to develop his talents with re-
gard to painting as he does with regard to literature.
e must remember that it is more natural for him to
develop towards the latter; hence he requires a more
especial culture if he would understand the former.

Of course, let it not be taken that I wouldn’t prefer
to hear intelligent people talking about pictures rather
than uniuntelligent. There are certain principles com-
mon to all the arts with which the man who under-
stands Shakespeare would be more likely to be acquainted
than he who never rises above 7'ut-Buts. Yet even on
this point one may have qualms of conscience when one
sees the things that in his time such a man as Edmund
Burke thought excellent.

The moral of the business is: in the first place our
education is, as the article of last week points out,
blameable (“ Imaal ’’ has pointed out the limited idea
of education we have); in the second place anyone writ-
ing about art in Ireland should write simply as possible,
Ireland being a child as regards art. Of course, there is
sad stuff written and said about art, but there is noth-
ing in the world about which the same kind of stuff
